email.subject: Daily Journey: Walker Miles on www.skechers.com

review.raw_markdown:
---

# Walker Miles — Skechers.com Mobile Journey Review
**Date:** March 28, 2026 | **vs. Yesterday:** 3/10

---

## 1. Executive Summary

Some genuine bright spots today — Men's content is actually leading with Slip-ins, which is exactly what I want to see. But two critical pages — Login and Cart — loaded completely blank. Can't sign into my Skechers Plus account, can't see my cart. That's the same broken core as yesterday. The browsing experience improved; the buying experience is still dead on arrival.

---

## 2. Business Impact Score: 4/10

Up one point from yesterday. The Men's category and product browsing are solid for someone like me. But a loyalty member who can't log in, can't check his rewards, and can't see his cart is not a customer who completes a purchase. The needle moved, but the wound is still open.

---

## 3. What's Working

- **BOGO 50% OFF is visible everywhere.** Top banner on every page. I'm price-conscious — I noticed it immediately and it kept me interested.
- **Men's Category nails it for me.** The page opens with "Slip-ins" and "Wide Width" as the very first featured links. That's *my* content, front and center. Whoever set that up understood what men in my age bracket are shopping for.
- **Men's Shoes page leads with Hands Free Slip-Ins.** First tile in the product grid. 620 results. I felt like I was in the right place.
- **Product page is clean and readable.** "$68.00" in large type, BOGO deal reinforced in red below the price, 5 stars with 48 reviews. I can read all of that without squinting or zooming. The shoe photo is big and clear.
- **ADD TO CART button is impossible to miss.** Full-width, bold blue, sticky at the bottom. My arthritic thumbs appreciate a button that big.
- **Size grid is reasonably tappable.** The size boxes aren't tiny — I could tap them without my reading glasses.

---

## 4. What's Weak

- **Login page: complete blank.** I navigated to the login page and got a white screen. Nothing. No form, no error message, no way to sign into my Skechers Plus account. If I can't log in, I can't see my rewards points, my member pricing, or my order history. This is the same failure as yesterday — no fix.
- **Cart page: complete blank.** After the add-to-cart step, the cart loaded as an entirely white page. I have no idea if my item is in there. No product, no total, no checkout button. Again — same as yesterday.
- **Add to Cart required a size selection I didn't make.** The page showed "Please select a size" in red. The automation skipped size selection, but even setting that aside, there's no instruction before the grid telling you that you MUST pick a size before tapping the button. A first-time visitor could tap ADD TO CART, get the error, and feel confused.
- **Search for "slip in" shows Women's first.** 1,017 results came up — good count — but the first two visible products were both labeled "Women's." I searched from a Men's mindset and got Women's shoes at the top. No men's filtering was applied. For me, that's immediately off-putting.
- **No personalized greeting or logged-in state visible anywhere.** Even on pages that loaded, nothing acknowledged I was (attempting to be) a Skechers Plus member. No "Welcome back, Walker" moment.

---

## 5. Recommendations

1. **Fix the login and cart pages immediately.** These are P0 failures. A loyalty member who can't sign in and can't view their cart has zero reason to buy. This is the second consecutive day these pages are blank — it should have been caught and escalated yesterday.
2. **Make search gender-aware.** When I'm shopping in Men's and then search "slip in," show me men's results first. The system knows I navigated through Men's — use that context.
3. **Add a pre-selection prompt on product pages.** Before the size grid, add one line: "Select your size to add to cart." Don't make me guess why the button didn't work.
4. **Surface the Plus membership benefit on every page.** I'm a loyalty member. The "Free Shipping with Skechers Plus" sub-banner appeared on Step 2, then disappeared. That benefit should follow me throughout the journey — especially on the product page near the price.
5. **Confirm cart addition with a visible toast or confirmation.** Even if the cart page itself were working, there's no in-context confirmation that my item was added. Add a brief overlay or banner: "Added to Cart — View Cart."

---

## 6. Bottom Line

Skechers clearly knows their Men's Boomer customer when it comes to merchandising. Slip-ins up front, Hands Free highlighted, ArchFit promoted — that's all right. But I can't log in to my loyalty account and I can't see my cart. Those aren't minor inconveniences; they're the two things that turn a browser into a buyer. Same critical failures as yesterday, two days in a row. I'd give up and call the 800 number, and that's not a good sign.

**Score: 4/10** (+1 from yesterday's 3/10)

---

## 7. Evidence

### Step 1 — Homepage
Clean, loads fast. BOGO 50% OFF banner is prominent. "Spring Style Guide" hero with a family photo — generic, not men-specific but not off-putting. "Shop Now" button is large and easy to tap. Sub-banner beneath the logo shows the deal. No friction here, felt welcoming enough.

### Step 2 — Dismiss Popups
No aggressive popup appeared — the sub-banner simply rotated from the BOGO message to a "Free Shipping with Skechers Plus — Join Now for free" message. That's subtle and non-intrusive. Easy to handle. No accidental tapping required. I appreciated not being assaulted by a giant modal.

### Step 3 — Log In
**Blank white screen.** Completely empty page. No login form, no error, no loading indicator. As a Skechers Plus member, this is my single biggest pain point. I have rewards points. I want to see my member status. I get nothing. Same failure as yesterday — unacceptable for a second day.

### Step 4 — Men's Category
This was the highlight of my trip. First things I saw: "Slip-ins" and "Wide Width" — the two categories that matter most to me. Then an ArchFit promotion. Skechers understands what men my age are shopping for. This page felt like it was built for me. Touch targets on those category labels were large enough. Text was readable.

### Step 5 — Men's Shoes
"Men's Shoes" heading, supportive description copy ("comfort, innovation, style..."), then the product grid with "Hands Free Slip-Ins" as the very first tile. That's what I came for. 620 results is plenty. Filter and Sort By controls were visible. This page gets a thumbs up from me.

### Step 6 — View Product (Contour Foam Shorva)
Price ($68.00) was easy to read. BOGO deal shown in red just below the price — I clocked it immediately. 48 reviews, 5-star rating — builds trust. Product name is clear. The shoe photo was large and detailed. I could see what I was buying. This page felt honest and direct, the way I like shopping.

### Step 7 — Add to Cart
The size grid showed all available sizes (6.5–14.0). Tapping them looked manageable — the boxes weren't tiny. Automation didn't select a size, so the red "Please select a size" warning appeared. The large blue ADD TO CART button at the bottom is exactly right — sticky, full-width, impossible to miss. If I'd selected my size, this would have worked fine. The Ship It / Check in-store options were a nice touch.

### Step 8 — View Cart
**Blank white screen.** Same complete failure as login. No cart items shown, no total, no checkout path. At this point in my journey I'm completely stuck. I've picked a shoe I liked, gone to add it, and now have no way to confirm it's saved or proceed to buy. This kills the transaction.

### Step 9 — Search ("slip in")
1,017 results — strong signal that the search index knows what Slip-ins are. But the first two visible products were both labeled "Women's." As a man who typed "slip in" looking for men's shoes, seeing women's results immediately felt wrong. It made me feel like I had to do extra work to find what I wanted. There should be a men's default or the system should infer gender from session context.

---

## Technical Audit

## Technical Summary

Audit covers an 8-step mobile purchase journey (Homepage → Login → Men's Category → Men's Shoes → View Product → Add to Cart → View Cart → Search). Key findings: the Login page is intercepted by a bot-detection challenge (KPSDK/PerimeterX), returning a bare HTML skeleton instead of the actual page. Cart also received a 429 block. 31 axe violations across the journey, concentrated on the View Product page. A third-party Stylitics widget has a CORS misconfiguration. LCP and CLS data were not captured for any step.

---

## Accessibility

**31 violations — 4 critical, 5+ serious, multiple moderate.**

**Critical (WCAG A failures):**
- `aria-required-parent` (Homepage): ARIA role elements missing required parent container roles — invalid DOM structure for assistive tech
- `aria-required-children` (View Product): ARIA composite roles missing required child roles
- `aria-valid-attr-value` (View Product): ARIA attributes with invalid values — causes screen reader misinterpretation
- `button-name` (View Product): One or more `<button>` elements have no discernible text (no label, no `aria-label`, no visible text content)

**Serious:**
- `link-name` (Homepage): Links with no accessible name — screen readers cannot describe navigation targets
- `aria-dialog-name` (View Product): ARIA dialogs/alertdialogs missing `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby`
- `aria-input-field-name` (View Product): ARIA input fields without accessible names
- `color-contrast` (View Product): Foreground/background contrast below WCAG AA threshold (4.5:1 for normal text)

**Moderate (landmark/heading structure — systemic):**
- `landmark-no-duplicate-main`: Multiple `<main>` elements present on Homepage — invalid document structure
- `landmark-complementary-is-top-level`: `<aside>` elements nested inside other landmarks (Homepage, Men's Shoes) — must be top-level or direct children of `<body>`
- `landmark-main-is-top-level`: `<main>` not a direct child of `<body>`
- `landmark-unique`: Non-unique landmarks without differentiating labels
- `heading-order`: Non-sequential heading hierarchy (e.g., h1 → h3) on Homepage
- `region`: Page content outside any landmark region

The landmark violations appear templated — same patterns repeat across Homepage and category pages, indicating a shared layout component issue.

---

## SEO

**Login page returns an empty challenge document.** The intercepted `/login/` response is `<html><head></head><body>[KPSDK script only]</body></html>` — zero indexable content. If Googlebot hits this page under similar rate-limit conditions, it indexes a blank page.

**Bot detection on `/cart/`**: Same risk for the cart URL if crawled.

**No evidence of structured data** in the truncated HTML samples (no JSON-LD, no schema.org markup visible). Product pages typically benefit from `Product`, `BreadcrumbList`, and `Review` schema.

**`lang="en"`** present on all real page responses — correct.

**Placeholder GTM tag** (`G-1234567890`) visible in source on Homepage and Men's Category: `gtag/destination?id=G-1234567890`. This is a test/placeholder GA4 property ID left in production markup — it will fire analytics events to a non-production property.

---

## Performance

| Step | TTFB | LCP | CLS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Homepage | 30ms | — | — |
| Log In | 157ms | — | — |
| Men's Category | 60ms | — | — |
| Men's Shoes | 49ms | — | — |
| View Product | 91ms | — | — |
| Add to Cart | 91ms | — | — |
| View Cart | 170ms | — | — |
| Search | 36ms | — | — |

TTFB values are acceptable (sub-200ms across all steps). Login (157ms) and Cart (170ms) are the highest — both also received 429s, suggesting the elevated TTFB reflects the challenge handshake overhead.

**LCP and CLS not captured** — these are the most user-impactful Web Vitals and are absent from the dataset. Cannot assess render performance.

**Third-party script payload (Homepage/Category pages):** 15+ async tracking scripts fire on each page load:
- TikTok Pixel (2 scripts)
- Google Tag Manager + 4 gtag destinations
- Bing Ads (`bat.js`)
- Reddit Pixel
- Quantum Metric
- Monetate (2 scripts)
- Pinterest (`core.js`)
- Yahoo (`ytc.js`, loaded twice)
- Impact CDN
- Bloomreach (`br-trk-7927.js`)
- Go2SDK/Tune
- WebFont Loader (loaded from cdnjs, not self-hosted)

Each of these is `async` which prevents render-blocking, but the sheer volume creates main-thread contention and network saturation, particularly on mobile.

---

## Mobile Optimization

**Viewport height calculation via JS:** `--vh: 6.640000000000001px` is injected as an inline style on `<html>`. This is a common workaround for mobile browser viewport height instability (100vh including/excluding browser chrome). The floating-point precision (`6.640000000000001`) indicates a raw `window.innerHeight / 100` calculation — should be rounded to avoid sub-pixel rendering artifacts.

**Login page inaccessible on mobile:** The KPSDK challenge blocks the login flow entirely during this session. If the bot scoring thresholds don't distinguish automated testing from real mobile traffic on restricted networks (e.g., shared IPs, VPNs, corporate proxies), real users could hit the same wall.

---

## Console & Network Errors

**429 Rate Limiting (7 occurrences):**
- 5× on `/149e9513.../fp?x-kpsdk-v=j-1.2.291` — PerimeterX/KPSDK fingerprinting endpoint rejecting the session
- 1× on `/login/` — login page blocked
- 1× on `/cart/` — cart page blocked

The fingerprinting endpoint hitting 429 repeatedly within a single session suggests the bot scoring system triggered on the automated browser. The same pattern could affect real users on flagged IPs or with unusual browser configurations.

**Stylitics CORS errors (4 entries):**
```
Access to CSS stylesheet at 'https://web-assets.stylitics.com/style.css?base=hotspots&version=1f1e003a2c'
from origin 'https://www.skechers.com' has been blocked by CORS policy:
No 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' header is present
```
Both a `<link rel="stylesheet">` and an `XMLHttpRequest` fetch of the same Stylitics stylesheet are CORS-blocked. The XHR attempt (likely a programmatic style injection fallback) also fails. The Stylitics "Shop the Look" widget will render without its styles — broken UI on affected pages.

---

## Recommendations

**Critical — fix immediately:**

1. **Fix `button-name`, `aria-required-children`, `aria-valid-attr-value`, `aria-dialog-name` on View Product** — these are WCAG A/AA failures that render the product page non-functional for screen reader users. Audit the size selector, image gallery, and modal components specifically.

2. **Fix `link-name` on Homepage** — unnamed links (`<a>` with only icon children) need `aria-label` or visible text. Common in icon-only nav and social links.

3. **Remove or replace placeholder GTM property `G-1234567890`** — currently firing analytics events to an unknown/test GA4 property in production.

4. **Coordinate with Stylitics to fix CORS headers** — `web-assets.stylitics.com` must add `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://www.skechers.com` (or `*`) to stylesheet responses. Alternatively, proxy or self-host the stylesheet.

**High priority:**

5. **Fix landmark structure sitewide** — the duplicate `<main>`, nested `<aside>`, and non-unique landmarks are templated issues. Fix the shared layout component to produce a single top-level `<main>` and ensure `<aside>` elements are not nested inside `<article>` or `<section>`.

6. **Fix color-contrast on View Product** — identify the failing element(s) (likely sale price, badge text, or secondary copy) and adjust to meet 4.5:1.

7. **Instrument LCP and CLS capture** — the current monitoring setup does not collect these. Use the PerformanceObserver API or a RUM provider to capture them; TTFB alone is insufficient for Core Web Vitals assessment.

**Medium priority:**

8. **Audit bot detection thresholds** — the repeated 429s on the fingerprinting endpoint (`fp?x-kpsdk-v`) within a single session suggests overly aggressive scoring. Work with the WAF/bot-detection vendor to tune thresholds so legitimate mobile users on shared network infrastructure are not blocked, particularly at `/login/` and `/cart/`.

9. **Consolidate third-party tracking through GTM** — at least 6 of the 15+ tracking scripts could be loaded via the existing GTM container rather than as separate `<script>` tags, reducing request overhead and centralizing tag governance.

10. **Fix floating-point `--vh` calculation** — `Math.round(window.innerHeight / 100 * 10) / 10` avoids sub-pixel precision artifacts.
